key_facts: Cells communicate to sense environment, send orders, heal wounds, and develop
Yeast mating factor causes a and alpha cells to elongate toward each other before fusing
common_confusions: Students may think communication only matters in multicellular organisms — yeast example shows it is universal
exam_traps: Signal transduction converts extracellular signal to intracellular response — not just transmission

key_facts: Contact-dependent signaling uses membrane-bound signal molecules
Delta-Notch system: Delta on signaling cell binds Notch on adjacent cell
Causes lateral inhibition and cell specialization
common_confusions: Delta is membrane-bound, NOT secreted — requires direct cell contact
Contact-dependent vs paracrine: key difference is whether signal is secreted
exam_traps: Delta-Notch is contact-dependent, not paracrine — Delta never leaves the membrane

key_facts: Paracrine signals diffuse locally in the neighborhood of the secreting cell
Called local mediators
Functions: regulate inflammation, control proliferation in healing wounds
common_confusions: Paracrine vs endocrine: paracrine stays local, endocrine enters bloodstream
exam_traps: Paracrine signals do NOT enter the bloodstream — this is the defining distinction from endocrine

key_facts: Endocrine cells secrete hormones into bloodstream
Pancreas secretes insulin to regulate glucose uptake in cells throughout the body
Insulin binds receptor on target cell, opens glucose channel
common_confusions: Endocrine vs paracrine distance distinction
Insulin acts on many cell types simultaneously via bloodstream
exam_traps: Endocrine signaling is slow to reach all targets but acts on many cells; synaptic is fast and specific

key_facts: Neuron sends electrical signal down axon to nerve terminal
Neurotransmitters released into synapse, bind target cell receptors
Fast and spatially precise — private lines
common_confusions: Synaptic vs paracrine: synaptic is directed through nerve terminal; paracrine diffuses broadly
exam_traps: Synaptic signaling uses neurotransmitters, NOT hormones — different molecule class and mechanism

key_facts: Reception: target cell detects extracellular signal via receptor
Transduction: converts signal via relay cascade to actionable form
Response: altered metabolism, cell shape, or gene expression
Transduction functions: relay, amplify, integrate, distribute, feedback control
common_confusions: Students confuse relay (passing signal) with transduction (converting signal)
All three phases are sequential and distinct
exam_traps: Transduction is conversion, not just passing — relay is one sub-function of transduction

key_facts: Cell-surface receptors: for hydrophilic signals that cannot cross membrane
Intracellular receptors: for small hydrophobic signals that freely cross membrane
Extracellular signal molecules bind one or the other depending on solubility
common_confusions: Intracellular receptor is inside the cell, not extracellular
The signal molecule for intracellular receptors still originates outside the cell
exam_traps: 'Intracellular receptor' describes receptor location, not signal origin — the signal still comes from outside

key_facts: Cortisol is a steroid hormone — crosses plasma membrane freely
Binds nuclear receptor protein in cytosol
Conformational change activates receptor
Receptor-cortisol complex translocates to nucleus
Binds regulatory region of target genes → transcription activated
Affects protein, carbohydrate, lipid metabolism in most tissues
common_confusions: Nuclear receptor starts in cytosol, not nucleus
Cortisol pathway is a slow response (gene expression) not fast
exam_traps: Cortisol's receptor is in the cytosol before ligand binding — only enters nucleus after forming the active complex

key_facts: Three classes: ion-channel-coupled, G-protein-coupled (GPCR), enzyme-coupled
Ion-channel-coupled: ligand opens ion channel directly — very fast (ms)
GPCR: 7 transmembrane helices; activates G protein GDP→GTP switch; GTPase returns to OFF
Enzyme-coupled: receptor dimerization activates intrinsic catalytic domain OR recruits associated enzyme; signal often a dimer
common_confusions: GPCRs do not have kinase activity — G protein is the switch
Enzyme-coupled receptors have kinase activity — students mix these up
Ion-channel-coupled is the only class that directly controls ion flow
exam_traps: GPCR vs enzyme-coupled: GPCR activates G protein, enzyme-coupled activates kinase — completely different downstream mechanisms

key_facts: Two switch types: phosphorylation (kinase ON, phosphatase OFF) and GTP-binding (GTP ON, GDP OFF)
Protein kinase transfers phosphate from ATP; protein phosphatase removes it
GTP-binding proteins turn ON with GTP binding, intrinsic GTPase turns them OFF
common_confusions: Kinase = ON (adds phosphate); phosphatase = OFF (removes) — students reverse this
GTP-binding switch is separate from phosphorylation switch
exam_traps: Protein kinase adds phosphate (ON); protein phosphatase removes (OFF). Never reverse.

key_facts: Cascade: receptor → scaffold proteins → intracellular signaling molecules → small messenger molecules → effectors
Amplification: one receptor activation triggers many downstream events
Effectors: metabolic enzymes, cytoskeletal proteins, transcription regulators
common_confusions: Amplification is a property of cascades — one signal does not equal one response molecule
Scaffold proteins organize cascade components for efficiency
exam_traps: Amplification means one signal → thousands of downstream events, not a 1:1 relationship

key_facts: Relay, amplify, integrate, distribute, feedback control
Integrate: multiple pathway inputs merge at one protein
Distribute: one receptor branches to multiple downstream pathways
Feedback control: product Y inhibits upstream X (negative feedback)
common_confusions: Feedback regulation occurs within transduction cascade, not after the response
exam_traps: Feedback regulation is part of transduction — not a fourth phase of signal transduction

key_facts: Same signal molecule can produce different responses in different cell types
Acetylcholine: decreased firing in heart pacemaker, secretion in salivary gland, contraction in skeletal muscle
Response determined by which receptors and downstream pathways are present in the cell
common_confusions: Students assume one signal = one universal response; receptor identity determines response
exam_traps: The signal molecule does not determine the response — the receiving cell's receptor and signaling machinery does

key_facts: Different combinations of extracellular signals lead to survive, grow and divide, differentiate, or apoptosis
Cells integrate multiple concurrent inputs before committing to a response
common_confusions: Cell fate is not determined by a single signal alone — combinations matter
exam_traps: A single signal molecule does not usually determine life vs. death — it is the combination of signals that determines fate

key_facts: Fast responses: < sec to mins; alter existing protein function; examples: muscle contraction, enzyme secretion
Slow responses: mins to hrs; require altered transcription and new protein synthesis; examples: cell growth, differentiation
common_confusions: Fast response does NOT require new protein synthesis
Both types are initiated by the same extracellular signal, different downstream pathways
exam_traps: Fast responses alter protein function; slow responses alter gene expression — mechanistically distinct

key_facts: HIPPO pathway controls organ size by regulating proliferation and apoptosis
YAP and TAZ are transcriptional co-activators; nuclear = pro-growth
Low cell density: YAP/TAZ unphosphorylated → nuclear → proliferation
High cell density: DCH S1/2 and FAT4 activate kinase cascade → phosphorylate YAP/TAZ → cytoplasmic retention by 14-3-3 → no proliferation
Fruit fly mutant with activated HIPPO: massive wing disc overgrowth (Huang et al., 2005, Cell)
Mouse liver: inducible YAP increases liver/body from ~5% to ~17% in 2 weeks; reversible; tumors at 8 weeks (Dong et al., Cell, 2007)
common_confusions: High density ACTIVATES HIPPO kinase cascade which INACTIVATES YAP/TAZ — students reverse this
14-3-3 sequesters phosphorylated YAP/TAZ in cytoplasm
Mouse liver data shows reversibility of size control
numeric_values: Liver/body ratio after 2 weeks YAP induction ~17%
exam_traps: High cell density = HIPPO ON = YAP/TAZ phosphorylated = cytoplasmic = no proliferation. Never say high density activates YAP.
